**OCP Project Deschutes**

Redfish, AI Control & Four-Tier Data Center Management

A Complete Teaching Reference

Prepared for: Scott Tomsu

ADC Solutions / Trappey's Compute Center

May 2026 (Rev 1.3 update: 2026-06-10)  |  CONFIDENTIAL

| PURPOSE: This document explains — from first principles — how Redfish works, how it maps to the OCP Project Deschutes CDU, and how artificial intelligence can manage all four tiers of a modern AI data center: IT hardware, cooling distribution, facility systems, and outside environmental monitoring. Every technical term is defined in plain language. |
| :---- |

# **REVISION 1.3 — ALIGNMENT TO THE 2026.1 PLATFORM BASELINE (2026-06-10)**

This revision brings the v1.2 teaching reference up to ADC's current
standards as set by **MGN-TEL-001 Rev 0.1** (Unified Telemetry &
Observability BoD), the **ADC-SYS-001 system registry** (`system/`),
and the 2026-06-10 OCP/DMTF currency check
(`ops/research/2026-06-10-ocp-spec-currency.md`). The teaching body
below is preserved; where it differs from this section, **this section
and MGN-TEL-001 govern.**

| # | v1.2 said | Current standard (Rev 1.3) |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| 1 | No Redfish release baseline stated (written against the 2025.4-era bundle) | Build on **DMTF 2026.1 bundle / Specification DSP0266 v1.24.0** (official date 2026-04-02; still latest as of 2026-06-10). Pin the schema tag before bridge/validator work (TEL-001). |
| 2 | Leak detection = detector state only | **LeakDetector v1.6.0** adds Voltage/CurrentAmps; **Environmental registry v1.5.0** adds **coolant-connector pair-reversal** messages (catches supply/return cross-connection at commissioning) and **dew-point** messages (condensation risk) — subscribe to both. These post-date v1.2 and are a direct reason to move to 2026.1. |
| 3 | N+1 described in prose | Encode with **Redundancy v1.7.0** MinNumNeededForFaultTolerance and **CoolingUnit v1.5.0** CoolingUnitRedundancy — same pattern used for the genset 4-of-5 on the power island. |
| 4 | 1 s / 5 s / 60 s polling rates | Formal cadence classes **P / A / B / C / D** per MGN-TEL-001 §9, including Class D compliance data to an immutable historian. Enable **Sensor v1.12.0** PeakIntervalReading / LowestIntervalReading on transient-critical points so load-step extremes between samples are never lost. |
| 5 | Bridge polls; AI reads the database | Prefer **streaming**: TelemetryService MetricReportDefinitions push per cadence class; TelemetryData for bulk pulls; `clientcontext` (Spec v1.24.0) for incremental collection reads; EventService subscriptions for alarms. **AggregationSource now federates Modbus sources directly** — pilot it before writing custom bridge code (TEL-AGG). |
| 6 | Four tiers (IT / CDU / facility / external) | The four-tier model remains valid as the **cooling + compute teaching view**. The platform standard is MGN-TEL-001's **five domains and six layers**, which add **D1 — the power island** (gensets, grid-forming BESS, 4.16 kV switchgear, SCR) under the same bridge pattern, plus the §10 cross-domain load-step correlation that joins GPUs, BESS, gensets, and the CDU/TCS on one clock. |
| 7 | No common-time requirement | A single **PTP/NTP-disciplined time base is mandatory** [L] — without it, cross-domain correlation is meaningless (TEL-TIME). |
| 8 | Validation = Redfish-Service-Validator | Conformance gate = **Service + Protocol + Interop validators** against the pinned 2026.1 schemas **plus a site Interoperability Profile** (DSP0272 1.10.0) per asset class — pass/fail artifacts for customers and lenders (TEL-PROFILE). |
| 9 | HTTPS noted | Full posture per MGN-TEL-001 §13: HTTPS + RBAC north-bound, Purdue-style OT/IT segmentation with bridges as the only crossing, optional SPDM attestation, and the fail-safe rule — local control continues, north-bound goes stale, never the reverse. |
| 10 | POC Phases 1–6 (cooling + IT only) | Superseded by MGN-TEL-001 §14, which inserts the **power-island bridge** at Phase 3. Phase 1 is already implemented: the ADC-SYS-001 registry and the executable twin (`system/twin/`) run the §10 correlation in simulation today. |
| 11 | §8.1 lists depth 40.29″, weight 6,910 lb wet, primary ΔP 15–27 psi | ADC-SYS-001 registry (sourced from Deschutes §3.1) carries **depth 42.29″, 6,900 lb wet, facility ΔP basis 15–30 psi**. Discrepancy flagged (SYS-OI-07) — resolve against the final OCP drawing package; until then the registry governs ADC documents. |

Terminology bridge: Tier 1 = domain **D2** (compute), Tier 2 = **D3**
(cooling — CDU + TCS), Tier 3 = **D4** (facility), Tier 4 = **D5**
(external). **D1 (power island) has no counterpart in this document** —
see MGN-TEL-001 §4. The AI layers (rule → PID → ML, §5) and the
PLC-keeps-safety-authority rule are unchanged and are now platform-wide
law — this document's clearest contribution, extended verbatim to the
gensets and BESS.

# **SECTION 1: What Is Redfish?**

## **1.1  The Simple Explanation**

Redfish is a language that computers use to talk to data center hardware. Think of it like a universal remote control — instead of having a different remote for every piece of equipment, Redfish gives every device the same button layout and the same language.

Before Redfish existed, every hardware vendor (Dell, HP, Cisco, ABB, Belimo) had their own private language for their equipment. If you wanted to read the temperature of a server, you needed Dell's special software. If you wanted to read the speed of a pump, you needed a different tool entirely. Nothing talked to anything else automatically.

Redfish solves this by making every device speak one standardized language over a normal internet connection. A server, a CDU pump, a power distribution unit, and a cooling valve can all be read and controlled using the exact same method.

## **1.2  Key Definitions — Building Blocks of Redfish**

| REST API | REST stands for Representational State Transfer. An API (Application Programming Interface) is a way for software to talk to hardware or other software. REST specifically means that communication happens over standard web technology (HTTP — the same technology your browser uses). A REST API means: send a web request to an address, get data back. No special software required. |
| :---- | :---- |

| HTTP | HyperText Transfer Protocol. This is the foundation of the internet. When your browser loads a webpage, it uses HTTP. Redfish uses this same technology to talk to hardware. Instead of loading a webpage, you load a piece of equipment's data. |
| :---- | :---- |

| JSON | JavaScript Object Notation. This is the format that Redfish uses to package data. It looks like organized text with labels and values — for example: {"Temperature": 45.2, "Units": "Celsius"}. It is human-readable and machine-readable at the same time. |
| :---- | :---- |

| Schema | A schema is a rulebook that defines what data must look like. Imagine a form with labeled boxes — the schema defines what labels exist, what type of data goes in each box (a number, a word, true/false), and which boxes are required. In Redfish, DMTF publishes the schema so every manufacturer knows exactly how to format their data. This is why a pump from one vendor and a server from another can be read the same way. |
| :---- | :---- |

| DMTF | Distributed Management Task Force. This is the industry organization that owns and publishes the Redfish standard. They define the schemas (the rulebooks). Google, Microsoft, Dell, HPE, Intel, NVIDIA, and hundreds of other companies are members. Because Google is a member, the Project Deschutes CDU is designed to plug into this ecosystem. |
| :---- | :---- |

| OCP | Open Compute Project. A nonprofit organization founded by Meta (Facebook) that publishes open-source hardware designs for data centers. Google contributed Project Deschutes to OCP so that anyone in the world — including you — can build a compatible CDU using the published specification. |
| :---- | :---- |

| Endpoint | In networking, an endpoint is an address you can send a request to. In Redfish, every piece of hardware has a base endpoint (like a home address on the internet), and each piece of data has its own sub-endpoint (like a room number in that building). Example: /redfish/v1/Chassis/CDU1/Thermal is the endpoint for thermal data on CDU unit 1\. |
| :---- | :---- |

| Resource | In Redfish, a resource is any piece of hardware or data that has an address and can be read or controlled. A pump is a resource. A temperature sensor is a resource. A valve position is a resource. Each resource lives at its own endpoint. |
| :---- | :---- |

| GET / PATCH | These are HTTP commands. GET means: give me the current value. PATCH means: change a value to what I specify. To read pump speed: GET /redfish/v1/.../Pump. To change pump speed: PATCH /redfish/v1/.../Pump with the new speed value in the message body. |
| :---- | :---- |

| Modbus/TCP | Modbus is an older industrial communication protocol developed in 1979\. It is simpler than Redfish but less descriptive — it reads numbered registers instead of named resources. The Project Deschutes spec requires the CDU PLC to speak Modbus/TCP. Redfish is then implemented as a translation layer on top of Modbus, or directly in a more capable controller. |
| :---- | :---- |

| PLC | Programmable Logic Controller. An industrial computer designed to control physical equipment — pumps, valves, heaters. The Deschutes CDU has a PLC that reads sensors and controls the pumps and valve. It speaks Modbus/TCP natively. Adding a Redfish service layer makes it speak both languages. |
| :---- | :---- |

| BMC | Baseboard Management Controller. A small dedicated computer built into every server that monitors the server independently of the main processor. It reads temperatures, fan speeds, power consumption, and can reboot the server remotely. Modern BMCs commonly expose Redfish natively. Confirm the exact BMC firmware supplier for each OEM platform before naming a specific firmware package. |
| :---- | :---- |

| AMI MegaRAC | Firmware family made by American Megatrends for server BMCs. MegaRAC implements Redfish on supported platforms, but use on any specific NVIDIA rack should be treated as an OEM confirmation item unless supplier documentation confirms it. |
| :---- | :---- |

## **1.3  How Redfish Actually Works — Step by Step**

Here is a concrete example of Redfish in action. You want to know the current secondary loop supply temperature on a Deschutes CDU:

| Step | What Happens |
| :---- | :---- |
| 1\. Your software sends a GET request | GET https://192.168.1.50/redfish/v1/.../SecondaryCoolantConnectors/RackSupply |
| 2\. The CDU controller receives the request | The Redfish service running on the CDU controller (or a gateway device) receives the HTTP request over the network. |
| 3\. The controller reads the PLC via Modbus | Internally, the Redfish service queries the PLC Modbus register that holds the supply temperature sensor value. |
| 4\. The controller formats the response | The raw Modbus register value (e.g., 2152 \= 21.52°C after scaling) is formatted into a Redfish CoolantConnector response using sensor-excerpt fields. |
| 5\. Your software receives structured data | {"Name": "Secondary Loop Supply", "SupplyTemperatureCelsius": {"Reading": 21.52}, "Status": {"Health": "OK"}} |
| 6\. AI layer ingests the data | The AI control system receives this value along with hundreds of others from all four tiers and makes decisions accordingly. |

## **1.4  Redfish vs. Modbus — Why Both Matter**

The Project Deschutes spec mandates Modbus/TCP as the native PLC protocol. Redfish is the management layer above it. They serve different purposes and work together:

| Modbus/TCP | Redfish |
| :---- | :---- |
| Developed 1979 | Developed 2015 |
| Industrial standard, very reliable | IT/web standard, very descriptive |
| Reads numbered registers (e.g., Register 40001 \= 21.5°C) | Reads named resources (e.g., /ThermalEquipment/.../SupplyTemperatureCelsius) |
| No built-in security (used on isolated industrial networks) | HTTPS encryption, authentication, role-based access |
| No self-description — you need a manual to know what register 40001 means | Self-describing — the schema tells you exactly what every field means |
| What the CDU PLC speaks natively | What the data center management system expects |
| Fast, lightweight, deterministic (good for real-time control loops) | Slower but rich — better for monitoring, dashboards, AI ingestion |

# **SECTION 2: Redfish Cooling Schemas — Deschutes Mapped**

## **2.1  What Are Cooling Schemas?**

A Redfish schema for cooling is a published rulebook that defines exactly how cooling equipment should describe itself over the network. DMTF published the ThermalEquipment and liquid-cooling schemas to support modern liquid-cooled data centers. Most major Deschutes CDU functions can be represented using standard Redfish resources; internal subcomponents may need sensor links, control excerpts, or OEM extensions depending on how the controller exposes them.

Think of schemas as a standard wiring diagram for data. Just like electrical codes define how wires must be labeled and sized, Redfish schemas define how data must be labeled and formatted. Once you follow the schema, any Redfish-capable software in the world can read your CDU.

## **2.2  The Deschutes CDU — Component to Redfish Schema Map**

| Deschutes Physical Component | Redfish Schema Resource | Key Data Fields |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| CDU Unit (the whole machine) | ThermalEquipment/CDUs/{id} | Status, EquipmentType, RatedFlowLitersPerMinute, RatedPressurekPa, PrimaryCoolantConnectors, SecondaryCoolantConnectors |
| SPECK TOEMA065200 Pump (x2) | CDUs/{id}/Pumps/{id} | PumpSpeedPercent, SpeedRPM, SpeedControlPercent, PowerWatts or linked power sensor, Status, PumpType |
| ABB ACH580 VFD (x2) | Pumps/{id} plus OEM/Control details | SpeedControlPercent, ControlMode, SetPoint; VFD frequency/voltage/current may require OEM properties or linked sensors |
| Alfa Laval CB210-276AH HX (x3) | CoolingUnit metrics or OEM/InternalHeatExchangers; ThermalEquipment/HeatExchangers only if exposed as independent HX units | SupplyTemperatureCelsius, ReturnTemperatureCelsius, DeltaTemperatureCelsius, heat-transfer metrics |
| Belimo B6500 Valve (primary side) | PrimaryCoolantConnectors/{id} | ValvePositionPercent, ValvePositionControlPercent, ControlMode, ValveControl action where supported |
| Supply temperature sensor (secondary / IT side) | SecondaryCoolantConnectors/RackSupply | SupplyTemperatureCelsius, SupplyPressurekPa, FlowLitersPerMinute, DeltaPressurekPa |
| Return temperature sensor (secondary / IT side) | SecondaryCoolantConnectors/RackReturn | ReturnTemperatureCelsius, ReturnPressurekPa, DeltaTemperatureCelsius |
| Facility supply sensor (primary / facility side) | PrimaryCoolantConnectors/FacilitySupply | SupplyTemperatureCelsius, SupplyPressurekPa |
| Facility return sensor (primary / facility side) | PrimaryCoolantConnectors/FacilityReturn | ReturnTemperatureCelsius, ReturnPressurekPa |
| Flow meter (+/- 0.5%) | SecondaryCoolantConnectors/{id} or linked Sensor resource | FlowLitersPerMinute.Reading, RatedFlowLitersPerMinute |
| Leak detection (3 zones) | CDUs/{id}/LeakDetection with LeakDetectorGroups; source devices can live under Chassis/{id}/LeakDetectors/{id} | LeakDetectorGroups, Detectors array, DetectorState, Status |
| Expansion tank | CDUs/{id}/Reservoirs/{id} | FluidLevelPercent, CapacityLiters, InternalPressurekPa, Status |
| TCS piping / distribution loop (CDU to IT rack) | SecondaryCoolantConnectors/{rack}; linked Sensor resources; optional CDUs/{id}/Oem/TCSInterface/Branches/{id} | RackSupplyTemperatureCelsius, RackReturnTemperatureCelsius, FlowLitersPerMinute, DeltaPressurekPa, ValvePositionPercent, BalanceStatus |
| TCS coolant-quality and filter sensors | CDUs/{id}/Oem/TCSInterface/WaterQuality or linked Sensor resources | pH, ConductivityuScm, TurbidityNTU, FilterDeltaPressurekPa, TreatmentStatus |
| Heat Rejection System (HRS) / facility interface | PrimaryCoolantConnectors plus linked facility/BMS Sensor resources or CDUs/{id}/Oem/HRSInterface | FacilitySupply/ReturnTemperatureCelsius, FacilityFlowLitersPerMinute, HRSStatus, Fan/PumpStatus, WetBulbTemperatureCelsius |

![Schematic showing primary facility water loop, secondary IT rack loop, heat exchangers, pumps, valve, sensors, and leak zones.][image1]  
*Figure 1\. CDU layer with Technology Cooling System (TCS) secondary/rack loop and facility heat-rejection context.*

## **2.3  The Redfish Resource Tree — How It Is Organized**

Redfish organizes resources in a tree structure, like folders on a computer. Everything starts at /redfish/v1/ and branches from there. The tree below is a representative, schema-aligned model for a Deschutes CDU implementation. In this update, TCS means Technology Cooling System: the CDU-to-rack secondary distribution loop. TCS branch sensors are normalized into the CDU layer through standard SecondaryCoolantConnectors, linked Sensor resources, or an OEM TCSInterface. Outdoor heat-rejection equipment is treated as facility/HRS context rather than being called TCS.

![Corrected Redfish tree starting at /redfish/v1 and showing ThermalEquipment, CDUs, coolant connectors, pumps, filters, reservoirs, leak detection, and OEM extensions.][image2]  
*Figure 2\. Redfish/CDU resource model with a CDU-layer TCS rack-loop sensor interface.*

| KEY CONCEPT: Every item in this tree has its own web address (endpoint). Your AI system, your monitoring dashboard, or a simple Python script can request data from any of these addresses using a standard web request. No vendor-specific software required. |
| :---- |

## **2.4  Open Source Redfish Tools**

Because Redfish is an open standard, there are free, open-source tools available to implement it. These are the key tools for your POC:

| Tool | What It Does | Where to Get It |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| DMTF Redfish Mockup Server | Simulates a Redfish endpoint for testing without real hardware. Run it on a laptop and it behaves like a real CDU Redfish interface. | github.com/DMTF/Redfish-Mockup-Server |
| DMTF python-redfish-library | Open-source Python library for talking to Redfish devices. Read and write resources with simple Python code. | github.com/DMTF/python-redfish-library |
| OpenBMC | Open-source BMC firmware that implements Redfish natively. Used as a reference implementation and deployable on custom hardware. | github.com/openbmc/openbmc |
| Redfish Validator | Tests that your Redfish implementation correctly follows the published schemas. | github.com/DMTF/Redfish-Service-Validator |
| pymodbus | Open-source Python library for reading/writing Modbus registers — the bridge between your PLC and the Redfish layer. | github.com/pymodbus-dev/pymodbus |
| Node-RED | Visual programming tool for building data flows between Modbus, Redfish, MQTT, databases, and dashboards. No heavy coding required. | nodered.org |

# **SECTION 3: The Modbus-to-Redfish Bridge**

## **3.1  Why a Bridge Is Needed**

The Deschutes PLC speaks Modbus/TCP natively. The data center management system, AI platform, and external monitoring tools expect Redfish. The bridge sits between them and translates in real time.

Think of it like a translator at a meeting between a French speaker and a Spanish speaker. The bridge does not change what is being said — it just makes both sides able to understand each other. The PLC still runs its control loops in Modbus. The AI system reads and sends commands in Redfish. The bridge handles the conversion.

## **3.2  Bridge Architecture**

| Layer | Technology | Role |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| Physical Hardware | PLC (CompactLogix 5380), VFDs, CDU sensors, valve, TCS rack-loop sensors/manifold gateway, optional facility/HRS or BMS/SCADA feed | Does the actual CDU work and imports TCS rack-loop telemetry needed for hydraulic balancing |
| Native Protocol | Modbus/TCP registers (read/write) | PLC's language — fast, real-time, industrial |
| Bridge Software | Python service using pymodbus \+ FastAPI; optional BACnet/SCADA collector for TCS branch and facility/HRS points | Polls Modbus/TCS/HRS sources and exposes them as Redfish-style endpoints and sensor resources |
| Redfish Interface | HTTPS REST API with JSON responses | Standard language understood by all management software |
| AI Control Layer | Python/ML model consuming Redfish data | Makes intelligent decisions and issues commands back through Redfish |
| Monitoring Dashboard | Grafana, InfluxDB, or cloud DCIM platform | Human-readable visualization of all system data |

## **3.3  Key Modbus Registers for Deschutes CDU**

The following table shows the Modbus registers that the bridge must map to Redfish resources. Actual register addresses will depend on the PLC program — this represents the data points required by the spec:

| Data Point | Modbus Register Type | Redfish Mapping | Control Direction |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| Secondary supply temperature | Input register (read only) | SecondaryCoolantConnectors/RackSupply/SupplyTemperatureCelsius/Reading | Read |
| Secondary return temperature | Input register (read only) | SecondaryCoolantConnectors/RackReturn/ReturnTemperatureCelsius/Reading | Read |
| Primary facility supply temperature | Input register (read only) | PrimaryCoolantConnectors/FacilitySupply/SupplyTemperatureCelsius/Reading | Read |
| Primary facility return temperature | Input register (read only) | PrimaryCoolantConnectors/FacilityReturn/ReturnTemperatureCelsius/Reading | Read |
| Secondary supply pressure | Input register (read only) | SecondaryCoolantConnectors/RackSupply/SupplyPressurekPa/Reading | Read |
| Secondary return pressure | Input register (read only) | SecondaryCoolantConnectors/RackReturn/ReturnPressurekPa/Reading | Read |
| Flow rate (secondary loop) | Input register (read only) | SecondaryCoolantConnectors/RackSupply/FlowLitersPerMinute/Reading | Read |
| Pump 1 speed (RPM) | Input register (read only) | Pumps/Pump1/PumpSpeedPercent/SpeedRPM | Read |
| Pump 1 power (kW) | Input register (read only) | Pumps/Pump1/PowerWatts or linked Sensor.PowerWatts (convert kW x 1000\) | Read |
| Pump 2 speed (RPM) | Input register (read only) | Pumps/Pump2/PumpSpeedPercent/SpeedRPM | Read |
| Pump speed setpoint | Holding register (read/write) | Pumps/{id}/SpeedControlPercent/SetPoint or associated Control resource | Read \+ Write |
| Control valve position | Holding register (read/write) | PrimaryCoolantConnectors/{id}/ValvePositionControlPercent/SetPoint | Read \+ Write |
| Leak zone 1 status | Coil/discrete (read only) | CDUs/CDU1/LeakDetection/LeakDetectorGroups/{zone}/Detectors\[\]/DetectorState | Read |
| Pump control mode | Holding register (read/write) | Pumps/{id}/SpeedControlPercent/ControlMode | Read \+ Write |
| TCS supply temperature to rack manifold | Input register, remote I/O, or manifold gateway point (read only) | SecondaryCoolantConnectors/RackSupply/SupplyTemperatureCelsius/Reading or Oem/TCSInterface/Header/SupplyTemperatureCelsius | Read |
| TCS return temperature from rack manifold | Input register, remote I/O, or manifold gateway point (read only) | SecondaryCoolantConnectors/RackReturn/ReturnTemperatureCelsius/Reading or Oem/TCSInterface/Header/ReturnTemperatureCelsius | Read |
| TCS total loop flow rate | Input register or flow-meter gateway point (read only) | SecondaryCoolantConnectors/RackSupply/FlowLitersPerMinute/Reading or Oem/TCSInterface/Header/FlowLitersPerMinute | Read |
| TCS rack branch flow and Delta-T | Input register, smart manifold, or branch sensor gateway (read only) | CDUs/CDU1/Oem/TCSInterface/Branches/{rack}/FlowLitersPerMinute, SupplyTemperatureCelsius, ReturnTemperatureCelsius, DeltaTemperatureCelsius | Read |
| TCS remote/end-of-line Delta-P | Input register or differential-pressure sensor (read only) | CDUs/CDU1/Oem/TCSInterface/RemoteEndOfLine/DeltaPressurekPa/Reading | Read |
| TCS balancing valve position | Holding register or smart valve object (read/write if authorized) | CDUs/CDU1/Oem/TCSInterface/Branches/{rack}/ValvePositionPercent and ValvePositionControlPercent/SetPoint | Read; optional Write through PLC/BMS safety logic |
| TCS leak detection along piping | Coil/discrete input or leak controller point (read only) | CDUs/CDU1/LeakDetection/LeakDetectorGroups/{zone}/Detectors\[\]/DetectorState | Read |
| TCS coolant quality and filter Delta-P | Input register or analyzer/filter gateway (read only) | CDUs/CDU1/Oem/TCSInterface/WaterQuality/pH, ConductivityuScm, TurbidityNTU, FilterDeltaPressurekPa | Read |
| HRS/facility heat-rejection status (optional context) | BACnet object, SCADA point, or BMS gateway (read only unless approved) | PrimaryCoolantConnectors/FacilitySupply and FacilityReturn plus optional Oem/HRSInterface/Fans, Pumps, WetBulbTemperatureCelsius | Read; optional Write through BMS only |

![Flowchart showing PLC Modbus registers feeding a bridge service, which exposes Redfish API telemetry and accepts safe setpoint commands.][image3]  
*Figure 3\. Modbus-to-Redfish bridge path from PLC registers to Redfish telemetry and setpoints.*

# **SECTION 4: Four-Tier AI Control Architecture**

## **4.1  Why Four Tiers?**

A traditional data center manages each system independently. The servers react when they get hot. The CDU reacts when flow demand rises. The chiller plant reacts when the CDU calls for colder water. The power grid reacts when load increases. Each system is always behind — it is always reacting to what already happened.

A four-tier AI architecture changes this completely. The AI sees all four layers simultaneously and can predict demand before it happens. A workload spike is not a surprise — the AI saw the GPU utilization rising 30 seconds ago and already positioned cooling to match. This reduces thermal stress, improves PUE (Power Usage Effectiveness), and prevents emergency shutdowns.

## **4.2  The Four Tiers Defined**

| TIER 1 — IT Layer: The compute hardware inside the racks. GPU clusters, CPUs, memory, networking. Each server's BMC reports real-time thermal and power state via Redfish. This is where workload lives and where heat is generated. |
| :---- |

| TIER 2 \- CDU Layer: The Project Deschutes Cooling Distribution Unit plus the Technology Cooling System (TCS) rack-loop interface. This layer manages secondary loop flow from the CDU to the IT racks, primary loop exchange with the facility, and rack-level balancing feedback. The AI controls CDU pump Delta-P and primary valve/approach-temperature supervisory setpoints while reading TCS sensors such as rack supply/return temperature, rack Delta-T, branch flow, branch Delta-P, balancing valve position, leak zones, coolant quality, and filter Delta-P. |
| :---- |

| TIER 3 — Facility Layer: Everything inside the data center building but outside the CDU. Power distribution units (PDUs), uninterruptible power supplies (UPS), generators, building management system (BMS), and the primary chilled water plant connections. |
| :---- |

| TIER 4 \- External Monitoring Layer: Environmental and utility context outside the building that affects future capacity: weather forecast, wet-bulb trend, grid status, water availability, and other external constraints. Outdoor heat-rejection equipment is treated as facility/HRS context. The TCS itself is the CDU-to-rack secondary cooling loop and belongs in the CDU/Tier-2 control view. |
| :---- |

![Swimlane diagram with Tier 1 IT/BMC, Tier 2 CDU/PLC, Tier 3 facility systems, and Tier 4 external weather/TCS/grid constraints.][image4]  
*Figure 4\. Four-tier AI architecture with Technology Cooling System (TCS) in the CDU/Tier-2 layer.*

## **4.3  Tier 1: IT Layer — What the AI Reads**

Modern NVIDIA rack platforms expose BMC and Redfish management services. The AI reads the following from each rack via Redfish; confirm the exact BMC firmware provider with the OEM before specifying AMI MegaRAC by name:

| Data Point | Redfish Endpoint | AI Use |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| GPU junction temperature | /Chassis/{rack}/Thermal/Temperatures | Predict cooling demand in next 30-60 seconds |
| GPU power draw (per chip) | /Systems/{node}/Power/PowerControl | Calculate heat load being delivered to the CDU |
| Workload utilization % | /Systems/{node}/Processors/{id} | Anticipate load ramp — spinning up means more heat soon |
| Memory temperature | /Chassis/{rack}/Thermal/Temperatures | Secondary thermal indicator |
| Coolant inlet temperature (cold plate) | /ThermalEquipment/CDUs/{cdu}/SecondaryCoolantConnectors/{rack}/SupplyTemperatureCelsius | Direct feedback on CDU delivery performance |
| Coolant outlet temperature (cold plate) | /ThermalEquipment/CDUs/{cdu}/SecondaryCoolantConnectors/{rack}/ReturnTemperatureCelsius | Compute thermal delta across each rack |
| Fan speed (residual air cooling) | /Chassis/{rack}/Thermal/Fans | Indicator of residual thermal load not handled by liquid |
| Power capping status | /Systems/{node}/Power/PowerControl/PowerLimit | Know when compute is being throttled due to thermal |

## **4.4  Tier 2: CDU Layer — What the AI Controls**

The AI supervises the Deschutes CDU while also reading the CDU-layer Technology Cooling System (TCS) sensor interface. The PLC remains the deterministic control and safety authority; Redfish is used for supervisory telemetry, setpoint exchange, rack-loop balancing feedback, and normalized sensor ingestion.

![Block diagram of two closed loops: secondary Delta-P pump speed control and approach-temperature primary valve position control, both with PLC PID feedback and AI supervisory setpoints.][image5]  
*Figure 5\. Dual-loop control view: secondary Delta-P pump control and approach-temperature valve control.*

### **Control Loop 1: Secondary Loop Pressure Differential (Pump Speed)**

Goal: Maintain a constant pressure differential between the supply and return manifolds on the secondary (IT) side. This ensures every rack receives adequate flow regardless of individual rack demand variation.

* Default mode per spec: Fixed ΔP — pump speed modulates to maintain target pressure differential

* AI enhancement: The target ΔP setpoint itself is dynamically adjusted based on Tier 1 data

* If GPU utilization across the pod is rising rapidly → AI increases ΔP target preemptively

* If a rack goes offline or reduces load → AI reduces ΔP target to save pump energy

* Control actuator: pump speed setpoint through the ABB ACH580 VFD, modeled in Redfish as Pump SpeedControlPercent/SetPoint or the associated Control resource; the PLC converts this to a safe VFD command.

### **Control Loop 2: Approach Temperature (Primary Valve Position)**

Goal: Maintain a 3°C approach temperature between the facility supply temperature and the secondary loop supply temperature. The approach temperature is the gap between how cold the facility water is and how cold the water delivered to the racks is. A smaller gap means more efficient heat exchange.

* Default mode per spec: ΔT\_approach \= T\_secondary\_supply\_cold − T\_primary\_supply\_cold \= 3°C target

* Control actuator: Belimo B6500 primary/facility valve position, modeled through the relevant CoolantConnector ValvePositionControlPercent/SetPoint or an OEM valve-control endpoint, with the PLC enforcing limits.

* AI enhancement: When facility supply temperature rises or heat-rejection capacity begins to degrade, as detected from facility/HRS sensors and Tier 4 weather context, AI pre-opens the primary valve to compensate before approach temperature degrades.

* If the heat-rejection plant is at capacity, the AI uses facility/HRS telemetry to signal Tier 3 early, reduce non-critical load, and avoid waiting for a CDU temperature alarm.

## **4.5  Technology Cooling System (TCS) at the CDU Layer**

The TCS (Technology Cooling System) is the secondary liquid-cooling distribution loop from the Deschutes CDU to the IT rack manifolds and back. It includes supply and return piping, rack branch circuits, cold-plate connections, balancing valves, flow sensors, pressure sensors, temperature sensors, leak detection, filters/reservoirs, and coolant-quality points. It should be modeled in the CDU layer because it is the loop the CDU pumps and controls.  
This placement gives the AI direct feedback for hydraulic balancing: rack supply/return temperature, rack Delta-T, branch flow, branch Delta-P, valve position, and end-of-line pressure. Commands to balancing valves or pump Delta-P targets remain supervisory; the PLC and site safety logic enforce limits, leak responses, and approved control authority.

| TCS rack-loop sensor point | CDU-layer mapping | Why the AI needs it |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| CDU secondary supply header temperature and pressure | SecondaryCoolantConnectors/RackSupply or Oem.TCSInterface/Header | Confirms what the CDU is delivering into the TCS rack loop. |
| CDU secondary return header temperature and pressure | SecondaryCoolantConnectors/RackReturn or Oem.TCSInterface/Header | Shows total rack heat pickup and overall loop pressure drop. |
| Total TCS loop flow rate | SecondaryCoolantConnectors/RackSupply/FlowLitersPerMinute or linked Sensor | Verifies that the pump/Delta-P command is producing the required loop flow. |
| Rack branch flow and rack Delta-T | Oem.TCSInterface/Branches/{rack}/Flow, SupplyTemp, ReturnTemp, DeltaTemp | Supports rack-by-rack balancing and identifies starved or overcooled racks. |
| Remote/end-of-line Delta-P | Oem.TCSInterface/RemoteEndOfLine/DeltaPressurekPa | Confirms the worst-case rack still has enough differential pressure. |
| Balancing valve command and feedback | Oem.TCSInterface/Branches/{rack}/ValvePositionPercent and ControlPercent | Shows whether balancing is hydraulic, valve-limited, or control-limited. |
| Rack coolant inlet/outlet temperature | SecondaryCoolantConnectors/{rack} or rack-level linked Sensor resources | Provides direct thermal feedback from cold plates and rack manifolds. |
| Leak zones along TCS piping | CDUs/CDU1/LeakDetection/LeakDetectorGroups/{zone} | Protects the IT space and supports fast isolation of affected branches. |
| Coolant quality and filter Delta-P | Oem.TCSInterface/WaterQuality plus filter Delta-P Sensor resources | Detects fouling, particulate loading, or fluid problems that hurt balancing. |

## **4.6  Tier 3: Facility Layer — What the AI Monitors and Coordinates**

| System | Data Points Read | AI Action |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| Primary chilled water plant | Supply temp, return temp, flow rate, delta-T, chiller COP | Optimize CDU approach temp target based on chiller efficiency curve |
| Power Distribution Units (PDUs) | Phase voltages, currents, kW per branch, harmonics | Detect overloads before breakers trip; shift workloads across feeds |
| UPS systems | Battery state of charge, runtime remaining, bypass mode status | Alert AI to reduce compute load if UPS is degraded |
| Generators | Run status, fuel level, load %, voltage/frequency quality | If utility fails, AI coordinates load shedding to stay within generator capacity |
| Building Management System (BMS) | Room temperature, humidity, CRAC/CRAH status, fire suppression status | Holistic facility health monitoring; detect hot spots before rack-level alarms |
| Electrical meters (utility) | Real power, reactive power, demand charges, power factor | Optimize workload scheduling to reduce peak demand charges |

## **4.7  Tier 4: External Monitoring Layer — What the AI Watches Outside**

This is the layer most traditional data center management systems ignore, and it is valuable for predictive AI control. In this revision, the TCS is the CDU-to-rack secondary loop and remains in Tier 2\. Tier 4 focuses on the outside context that predicts facility/HRS capacity: weather, wet-bulb trend, wind, grid conditions, water availability, and forecasted environmental limits.

### **Outside Heat Rejection Context**

The primary cooling loop (facility side of the Deschutes CDU) ultimately rejects heat to the atmosphere. For most data centers this is done by cooling towers, dry coolers, or adiabatic coolers. These systems are the final thermal boundary — when they are at capacity, no amount of CDU optimization can compensate. The AI must know their status before it becomes a problem.

| HRS / external equipment | Facility/BMS or HRS sensor data | AI use |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| Cooling Tower | Basin temperature, approach to wet-bulb, fan speed, blowdown status, pH/conductivity/ORP/turbidity | Predict facility supply limits and compare HRS telemetry against weather forecasts. |
| Dry Cooler / Adiabatic Cooler | Inlet air temperature, outlet fluid temperature, fan speed, spray activation, effectiveness % | Monitor real-time heat-rejection effectiveness and alert when ambient conditions approach design limits. |
| Condenser Water Loop | Supply/return temps, flow rate, pump status, chemical treatment injection | Maintain optimal condenser water Delta-T for chiller efficiency. |
| Fluid Cooler (closed-circuit) | Coil inlet/outlet temps, fan speed, sump level | Detect fouling and reduced effectiveness before CDU performance degrades. |

### **Weather and Environmental Data**

| Data Point | Why It Matters | AI Response |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| Wet-bulb temperature | This is the MOST important external metric for data center cooling. Wet-bulb temperature is the lowest temperature achievable by evaporation — it is the physical limit of cooling tower performance. When wet-bulb rises, cooling towers cannot reject heat as effectively, which raises facility chilled water temperature, which forces CDU approach temperature to rise. | When wet-bulb forecast shows rise above 75°F (24°C), AI begins pre-positioning: increase CDU secondary loop temperature setpoint slightly, reduce non-critical workloads, verify backup cooling capacity is online and ready. |
| Dry-bulb (ambient) temperature | Affects dry cooler and air economizer performance directly. | Predict when air economizer hours are available (free cooling from outside air). |
| Relative humidity | Combined with dry-bulb, determines wet-bulb. Also affects adiabatic spray effectiveness. | Input to wet-bulb calculation and spray system efficiency model. |
| Wind speed and direction | Affects cooling tower plume behavior and cross-contamination between towers. | Adjust tower fan speeds to prevent recirculation. |
| Weather forecast (24-72 hour) | Allows truly predictive — not just reactive — thermal management. | Preemptively schedule maintenance windows during low ambient periods; warn operators of difficult cooling conditions before they occur. |
| Solar irradiance | Affects building envelope heat load and rooftop equipment performance. | Predict when afternoon solar gain will increase facility cooling load. |

### **Water Chemistry and Treatment Monitoring**

The Deschutes spec explicitly requires coolant pH and electrical conductivity monitoring. External-loop chemistry monitoring helps prevent scale, corrosion, biological growth, reduced heat transfer, and equipment failures.

* pH: target 7.0-8.5; deviation indicates corrosion risk.  
* Conductivity: dissolved solids indicator; high values mean scaling risk.  
* ORP: biocide-effectiveness signal; low ORP means biological-growth risk.  
* Turbidity: suspended-particle signal; high values imply filter loading and corrosion risk.  
* AI action: correlate chemistry with heat-transfer performance and alert before degradation.

# **SECTION 5: The AI Control Engine**

## **5.1  Three Levels of AI Intelligence**

A well-designed AI control system for a data center is built in three layers of increasing sophistication. You can implement the first layer immediately with simple logic, and add the deeper layers over time.

### **Level 1: Rule-Based Control (Implement First)**

These are hard-coded if-then rules. Not machine learning — just logic. This is how you build a safe, functional baseline before adding intelligence on top.

* IF secondary supply temperature \> 45°C THEN increase pump speed setpoint by 5%

* IF approach temperature \> 5°C (above 3°C target) THEN open primary valve by 10%

* IF any leak detector \= LEAK THEN alarm \+ emergency pump stop sequence

* IF Pump1 fault THEN enable Pump2 (N+1 failover)

* IF wet-bulb temperature \> 25°C THEN reduce maximum IT power by 10%

* IF utility grid voltage outside ±10% THEN switch to generator \+ reduce non-critical loads

### **Level 2: PID Control (Precise Loop Control)**

A PID controller is a mathematical formula used in almost every industrial control system. PID stands for Proportional-Integral-Derivative. It is more precise than simple rules because it considers not just the current error but how fast the error is changing and how long it has persisted.

| Proportional (P) | Responds to the current error. If the approach temperature is 2°C above target, open the valve proportionally to that error. Bigger error \= bigger response. |
| :---- | :---- |

| Integral (I) | Responds to accumulated error over time. If the approach temperature has been slightly above target for 5 minutes, the integral term gradually increases the valve opening until the error is eliminated. |
| :---- | :---- |

| Derivative (D) | Responds to the rate of change of error. If the approach temperature is rising rapidly, the derivative term acts immediately to prevent overshoot — it brakes the response before the target is passed. |
| :---- | :---- |

For the Deschutes CDU, two PID loops run simultaneously: one for pump speed (controlling secondary ΔP) and one for valve position (controlling approach temperature). The Allen-Bradley CompactLogix 5380 PLC has built-in PID function blocks — these are already in the hardware, just need to be configured.

### **Level 3: Machine Learning (Predictive Control)**

Machine learning elevates the system from reactive to predictive. Instead of responding to what is happening now, the AI predicts what will happen and acts in advance. Key ML approaches for this application:

| ML Technique | What It Does | Application in This System |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| Time Series Forecasting (LSTM / Transformer) | Learns patterns in historical data to predict future values. An LSTM (Long Short-Term Memory) neural network is particularly good at sequential data like temperature trends over time. | Predict secondary loop temperature 2-5 minutes ahead based on GPU utilization patterns. Allow AI to pre-position pump speed and valve before the temperature actually rises. |
| Anomaly Detection (Isolation Forest / Autoencoder) | Learns what normal looks like and flags deviations. Can detect subtle performance degradation weeks before a component fails. | Detect pump bearing wear (unusual vibration signature), heat exchanger fouling (efficiency slowly declining), valve stiction (valve not responding as expected), or leak precursors. |
| Reinforcement Learning (RL) | The AI learns optimal control policies by trial and error — receives a reward for good outcomes (low energy use, stable temperatures) and penalty for bad outcomes (thermal excursions, equipment stress). | Optimize the combined ΔP setpoint and approach temperature target across all CDUs in a pod simultaneously to minimize total facility cooling energy while maintaining IT thermal guarantees. |
| Digital Twin | A live virtual replica of the physical system, updated in real time with sensor data, used to simulate what-if scenarios before commanding real hardware. | Before making a major control change (reducing flow rate, changing setpoint), the AI runs the scenario in the digital twin first to verify it is safe. |

## **5.2  The Complete AI Data Flow**

Here is the complete data flow from physical sensor to AI decision to hardware action:

| Step | Action | Technology |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| 1 | Physical sensors read: CDU temperatures, pressures, flow rates, pump speeds, TCS rack-loop sensors, GPU utilization, facility/HRS capacity, facility power, and weather | Hardware sensors \-\> PLC Modbus registers; BMC Redfish endpoints; TCS manifold gateway; HRS/BMS gateway; weather API |
| 2 | Bridge polls all data sources at their respective rates (fast: 1-second for CDU control loops; medium: 5-second for IT/TCS rack loop; slow: 60-second for weather/facility/HRS) | pymodbus \+ python-redfish-library \+ BACnet/SCADA collector \+ REST weather API |
| 3 | All data written to time-series database with timestamps | InfluxDB or TimescaleDB |
| 4 | AI inference engine reads latest data window (e.g., last 5 minutes of all signals) | Python ML model (TensorFlow / PyTorch / scikit-learn) |
| 5 | AI generates setpoint recommendations: pump ΔP target, valve position, alerts | ML inference \+ rule-based safety overrides |
| 6 | Recommendations passed to control execution layer with safety checks | Safety envelope validation — never exceed physical limits |
| 7 | Control commands issued via Redfish PATCH requests to the bridge | HTTP PATCH → Modbus write → PLC actuates |
| 8 | Physical hardware responds: pump changes speed, valve adjusts, temperatures stabilize | ABB VFD \+ Belimo actuator \+ fluid dynamics |
| 9 | New sensor readings flow back into database — the loop continues | Continuous closed-loop operation |
| 10 | Dashboard displays all data; anomalies trigger alerts; reports auto-generate | Grafana \+ alerting rules \+ email/SMS/Slack |

# **SECTION 6: Proof of Concept — Build Plan**

## **6.1  POC Objectives**

* Demonstrate a Deschutes-spec CDU built from Google's published BoM operating in closed-loop mode

* Implement a Redfish API layer on the CDU PLC making it fully OCP-compatible at the software layer

* Demonstrate all four tiers of AI-assisted monitoring on a single dashboard

* Validate the Modbus-to-Redfish bridge with real hardware

* Establish a teachable, replicable reference architecture for future deployments

## **6.2  Hardware Requirements**

| Component | Spec / Part Number | Purpose in POC |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| Pumps (x2) | SPECK TOEMA065200 (or Magnatex / Sundyne HMD Kontro alternate) | Primary flow movers — N+1 configuration |
| Heat Exchangers (x3) | Alfa Laval CB210-276AH | Liquid-to-liquid heat transfer between loops |
| VFDs (x2) | ABB ACH580-31-065A-4 with \+B056 NEMA 12 option | Variable speed drive for each pump |
| Control Valve | Belimo B6500 with actuator | Primary loop flow modulation |
| PLC | Allen-Bradley CompactLogix 5380 | Control logic, PID loops, Modbus/TCP server |
| Flow Meter | Turbine or Coriolis type, ±0.5% accuracy | Secondary loop flow measurement |
| Pressure/Temp Sensors (x4) | Combined P+T sensors at each manifold | Supply/return sensing on both loops |
| Leak Detection (x3 zones) | Water-sensing rope cable \+ controller | Safety monitoring |
| IT Load Simulator | Variable orifice plate \+ immersion heater \+ flow controller | Simulates rack thermal load without real servers |
| Single Board Computer | Raspberry Pi 4 or industrial PC | Runs the Redfish bridge software and AI engine |
| Network Switch | Unmanaged Gigabit switch | Connects PLC, SBC, and monitoring laptop |
| TCS rack-loop sensor gateway / simulator | Modbus/TCP, BACnet/IP, or smart-manifold simulator with rack supply/return temp, branch flow, branch Delta-P, balancing valve position, leak zones, coolant quality, and filter Delta-P | Demonstrates CDU-layer rack-loop balancing telemetry without waiting for a full IT rack deployment |

## **6.3  Software Stack**

| Software Layer | Open Source Tool | Function |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| PLC Programming | Studio 5000 Logix Designer (Allen-Bradley) | Configure PID loops, Modbus server, I/O mapping |
| Modbus-to-Redfish Bridge | Python \+ pymodbus \+ FastAPI | Translates Modbus registers to Redfish JSON endpoints |
| Redfish Schema Validation | DMTF Redfish-Service-Validator | Confirms bridge output matches DMTF schemas |
| Time Series Database | InfluxDB (open source) | Stores all sensor data with timestamps for AI training |
| AI/ML Engine | Python \+ scikit-learn \+ TensorFlow | Level 2 PID \+ Level 3 predictive models |
| External Weather API | Open-Meteo (free, no API key required) | Provides wet-bulb, dry-bulb, humidity, wind forecast |
| Dashboard / Visualization | Grafana (open source) | Real-time display of all four tiers in one view |
| Alerting | Grafana Alerting \+ SMTP/Slack webhook | Notify operators of anomalies and predicted issues |
| DMTF Mockup Server | github.com/DMTF/Redfish-Mockup-Server | Test AI and dashboard before real hardware is connected |
| TCS Rack-Loop Data Collector | BACnet/IP library, pymodbus, MQTT, site SCADA connector, or Redfish/OEM Sensor bridge | Normalizes TCS branch/manifold sensors into the CDU-layer Redfish/AI namespace |

## **6.4  Implementation Sequence**

1. Phase 1 — Simulate: Run the DMTF Redfish Mockup Server on a laptop. Build the AI engine and dashboard against the simulated CDU. No hardware required. Validate all software logic.

2. Phase 2 — Bridge: Connect to the real PLC via Modbus. Deploy the Modbus-to-Redfish bridge on the Raspberry Pi or industrial PC. Validate that all Redfish endpoints return real sensor values.

3. Phase 3 — Control: Implement the PID loops in the AI engine. Issue PATCH commands through Redfish to control pump speed and valve position. Achieve closed-loop operation on the CDU with simulated IT load.

4. Phase 4 — Tier 1 Integration: Connect to NVIDIA rack BMCs via Redfish. Ingest GPU thermal and power data into the AI engine. Demonstrate predictive pump speed adjustment before GPU temperatures rise.

5. Phase 5 — Tier 4 Integration: Connect weather API. Demonstrate AI pre-positioning cooling when wet-bulb forecast rises. Connect outside heat rejection equipment sensors.

6. Phase 6 — ML Training: After 30+ days of operational data, train the LSTM forecasting model. Replace rule-based setpoint selection with ML-predicted optimal setpoints. Measure improvement in thermal stability and energy consumption.

# **SECTION 7: Complete Glossary**

| Term | Plain Language Definition |
| :---- | :---- |
| AI | Artificial Intelligence. Software that learns from data and makes decisions, rather than following only pre-written rules. |
| AMI MegaRAC | Firmware family made by American Megatrends for server BMCs. MegaRAC implements Redfish on supported platforms, but use on any specific NVIDIA rack should be treated as an OEM confirmation item unless supplier documentation confirms it. |
| API | Application Programming Interface. A standardized way for software to talk to hardware or other software. |
| Approach Temperature | The temperature difference between the facility supply water and the secondary loop supply water after passing through the heat exchanger. Target in Deschutes: 3°C. Smaller \= more efficient. |
| BMC | Baseboard Management Controller. A small computer built into every server that monitors it and speaks Redfish. |
| Bridge (Modbus-to-Redfish) | Software that reads Modbus registers from the PLC and presents the data as Redfish endpoints. |
| CDU | Cooling Distribution Unit. The machine that transfers heat from the server liquid loops to the facility chilled water system. |
| Closed-Loop Control | A control system where the output (e.g., pump speed) is continuously adjusted based on feedback from sensors (e.g., pressure differential). It corrects itself automatically. |
| DCIM | Data Center Infrastructure Management. Software platform that aggregates monitoring and control across an entire data center. |
| Delta-T (ΔT) | Temperature difference between two points. ΔT across a heat exchanger \= efficiency measure. ΔT across a rack \= heat load measure. |
| ΔP (Delta-P) | Pressure difference between two points. The pump maintains a target ΔP between supply and return manifolds to ensure adequate flow to all racks. |
| DMTF | Distributed Management Task Force. The organization that publishes and maintains the Redfish standard. |
| Dry-Bulb Temperature | Standard air temperature measured by a normal thermometer, not accounting for humidity. |
| Endpoint | A specific web address in a Redfish system where data can be read or written. |
| GET | An HTTP command meaning: read this data. Used by Redfish to retrieve sensor values. |
| HTTPS | Secure version of HTTP. Uses encryption. Redfish requires HTTPS for security. |
| HTTP | HyperText Transfer Protocol. The web communication standard that Redfish is built on. |
| JSON | JavaScript Object Notation. The text format used by Redfish to package data — human-readable key:value pairs. |
| LSTM | Long Short-Term Memory. A type of neural network that excels at learning patterns in time-series data (e.g., temperature trends). |
| Modbus/TCP | Industrial communication protocol used by the Deschutes CDU PLC natively. Reads/writes numbered registers over an Ethernet network. |
| ML | Machine Learning. A subset of AI where the system learns patterns from historical data rather than following only hand-written rules. |
| N+1 | Redundancy configuration where N units are required for full operation and one additional (the \+1) is on standby. Deschutes uses N+1 pumps. |
| OCP | Open Compute Project. Nonprofit organization that publishes open-source data center hardware designs. Project Deschutes is an OCP contribution by Google. |
| PATCH | An HTTP command meaning: change a value. Used by Redfish to send control commands to hardware. |
| PID Controller | Proportional-Integral-Derivative controller. A standard mathematical formula for precise closed-loop control used in the CDU pump and valve loops. |
| PLC | Programmable Logic Controller. Industrial computer that runs control logic and speaks Modbus/TCP. |
| POC | Proof of Concept. A small-scale working demonstration that validates a design before full deployment. |
| PUE | Power Usage Effectiveness. Ratio of total data center power to IT equipment power. Lower is better. AI cooling optimization directly improves PUE. |
| Redfish | DMTF open standard REST API for data center hardware management. The universal language that servers, CDUs, PDUs, and cooling systems use to communicate with management software. |
| Redmond | Google's Hot Aisle Containment (HAC) structural frame specification. Section 18 of the Project Deschutes document. Defines the physical structure, rack spacing, elevations, and manifold placement for Deschutes deployments. |
| REST | Representational State Transfer. An architectural style for web APIs that uses standard HTTP methods (GET, PATCH, POST, DELETE). |
| Schema | A published rulebook defining what data must look like — what fields exist, what types they are, which are required. DMTF publishes Redfish schemas so any manufacturer's hardware can be read the same way. |
| Secondary Loop | The IT side of the Deschutes CDU — the fluid loop that circulates from the CDU to the server racks and back. Carries heat from the servers to the CDU heat exchanger. |
| Primary Loop | The facility side of the Deschutes CDU — the building chilled water loop. Carries heat from the CDU heat exchanger to the outside heat rejection plant. |
| TCS | Technology Cooling System. The secondary IT cooling distribution loop from the CDU to the rack manifolds and back. It includes supply/return piping, branch circuits, balancing valves, rack-level flow/pressure/temperature sensors, leak detection, filters, and coolant-quality points. Outdoor cooling towers, dry coolers, and adiabatic coolers are heat-rejection/facility systems, not the TCS. |
| VFD | Variable Frequency Drive. Controls motor speed by varying the electrical frequency. The ABB ACH580 drives the SPECK pumps — changing VFD frequency changes pump RPM and therefore flow/pressure. |
| Wet-Bulb Temperature | The lowest temperature achievable by evaporative cooling. The fundamental limit of cooling tower performance. Critical external metric for AI thermal planning. |

# **SECTION 8: Quick Reference — The System at a Glance**

## **8.1  Deschutes CDU Key Specifications**

| Parameter | Value | Notes |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| Thermal capacity | 2,000 kW (2 MW) |  |
| Secondary loop flow rate | 500 GPM |  |
| Secondary loop pressure available | 80–90 psi | To racks |
| Primary loop pressure drop | 15-27 psi | Facility side; verify against final OCP spec revision and supplier submittal. *Rev 1.3: ADC registry carries 15–30 psi facility ΔP basis per Deschutes §3.1 (SYS-OI-07).* |
| Approach temperature target | 3°C | CDU HX performance target |
| Operating liquid temperature | 18–55°C |  |
| Input power (CDU) | 380–416 Vac, 3-phase, 50/60 Hz |  |
| Total power consumption | \~74 kW |  |
| Pump model | SPECK TOEMA065200 | Sealless mag-drive, N+1 |
| Pump power each | 37 kW |  |
| VFD model | ABB ACH580-31-065A-4 | 50 HP, IEEE 519 compliant |
| Heat exchanger | Alfa Laval CB210-276AH × 3 parallel | Dual-pass |
| Control valve | Belimo B6500 | Primary side |
| Control protocol | Modbus/TCP (IPv4 \+ IPv6 dual stack) | PLC native |
| Management protocol | Redfish (via bridge) | OCP/DCIM layer |
| CDU dimensions (W×H×D) | 65.25" x 91.44" x 40.29" | Public Deschutes technical material lists 40.29" depth; verify with final drawing package. *Rev 1.3: ADC registry carries 42.29" depth per Deschutes §3.1 — registry governs ADC documents until the drawing package settles it (SYS-OI-07).* |
| CDU weight (wet/dry) | 6,910 lbs / 5,310 lbs | Use supplier shipping and installation documents for final rigging. *Rev 1.3: ADC registry carries 6,900 lb wet per Deschutes §3.1 (SYS-OI-07).* |
| Coolants | DI water or PG25 |  |
| Leak detection | 3 zones, PLC-integrated |  |
| TCS sensor integration | Secondary rack-loop telemetry interface | Includes rack supply/return temps, branch flow, branch Delta-P, balancing valve position, leak zones, coolant quality, and filter Delta-P |
| TCS data protocol | Modbus/TCP, BACnet/IP, MQTT, SCADA, or Redfish/OEM Sensor bridge | Normalize TCS into CDU Redfish/AI model; PLC remains safety authority |

## **8.2  Four-Tier Summary**

| Tier | Scope | Primary Protocol | AI Role |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| Tier 1 — IT | Server racks, GPUs, BMCs | Redfish (native) | Predict heat load from compute state |
| Tier 2 \- CDU | Deschutes CDU, pumps, valve, HX, leak detection, TCS secondary/rack-loop sensors | Modbus/TCP \-\> Redfish bridge; TCS sensors via I/O, Modbus, BACnet, SCADA, or sensor bridge | Control Delta-P and approach temp; balance rack loop from branch feedback |
| Tier 3 — Facility | PDUs, UPS, generators, BMS, chiller plant | BACnet/IP, SNMP, Modbus, Redfish | Coordinate power and cooling capacity |
| Tier 4 \- External | Weather, wet-bulb forecast, grid, water availability, HRS constraints | REST APIs, weather station, utility feeds, SCADA context | Predict external constraints before they reduce facility/HRS capacity |

## **8.3  Control Loop Quick Reference**

| Loop | Actuator | Sensor Feedback | Default Target | AI Action |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| Secondary ΔP (pump speed) | ABB ACH580 VFDSpeedControlPercent | Supply pressure – Return pressure | Fixed ΔP setpoint | Dynamic setpoint based on GPU utilization forecast |
| Approach temperature (valve) | Belimo B6500 | ValvePositionControlPercent | T\_secondary\_supply \- T\_primary\_supply | 3°C approach | Pre-position valve based on facility/HRS telemetry and wet-bulb forecast |
| N+1 failover | Pump 2 enable | Pump 1 fault status | Pump 1 active | Predictive failure detection before fault occurs |
| TCS rack-loop balancing | Pump Delta-P target plus branch/balancing valves where installed | Rack supply/return temp, rack Delta-T, branch flow, branch Delta-P, valve feedback | Maintain sufficient flow and end-of-line Delta-P | Recommend balancing adjustments and optimize pump energy without starving racks |

*Revision note: Redfish liquid-cooling names in this revision are aligned to DMTF DSP2064 / DSP2046 terminology: CoolantConnector sensor excerpts use fields such as SupplyTemperatureCelsius, ReturnTemperatureCelsius, FlowLitersPerMinute, SupplyPressurekPa, ReturnPressurekPa, DeltaPressurekPa, PumpSpeedPercent, SpeedControlPercent, ValvePositionPercent, ValvePositionControlPercent, and LeakDetection/LeakDetectorGroups. Primary is treated as the facility side; secondary is treated as the IT/rack side, matching this document’s glossary.*

**Document Information**

| Field | Value |
| :---- | :---- |
| Document Title | OCP Project Deschutes — Redfish, AI Control & Four-Tier Data Center Management |
| Prepared By | Scott Tomsu |
| Date | May 2026 (Rev 1.3: 2026-06-10) |
| Based On | OCP-Specification-Deschutes v1.0 (Effective Feb 26, 2026\); DMTF Redfish 2026.1 / DSP0266 v1.24.0 (2026-04-02); MGN-TEL-001 Rev 0.1; ADC-SYS-001 registry |
| Classification | CONFIDENTIAL |
| Version | 1.3 \- Aligned to the 2026.1 platform baseline (see REVISION 1.3 section at top) |
| **Revision Update (1.3)** | Updated Redfish baseline to 2026.1/v1.24.0; added pair-reversal/dew-point/peak-dip/Redundancy v1.7.0 capabilities; reconciled four-tier model to MGN-TEL-001 five domains (adds D1 power island); cadence classes, common time base, conformance gate, security posture; flagged §8.1 numeric discrepancies vs ADC-SYS-001 registry (SYS-OI-07) |
| **Revision Update (1.2)** | Defined TCS as the CDU-to-rack Technology Cooling System; added balancing sensors and separated heat rejection into facility/HRS context |

| NOTE: This document is a teaching and engineering reference. All component specifications should be verified against the applicable OCP-Specification-Deschutes revision and supplier submittals. Open source software references are publicly available under their respective licenses. Redfish schemas are published by DMTF under open license. This revision defines TCS as the Technology Cooling System secondary distribution loop from CDU to racks and separates outdoor/facility heat-rejection telemetry into HRS/facility context. |
| :---- |
